1. # **Introduction and Context** {#introduction-and-context}

The problem of fraud detection is perhaps the most defined and challenging supervised live-world problem, Which presents few solutions. Cases of fraud have been recorded in the UK, which represent less than four percent of the transactions. Of all the records, and fraudsters adapt their strategies during the process. This level of severe The recurring issue of class imbalance and concept drift causes a perfectly trained model to silently degrade in performance. Except when monitored closely. As mentioned several times before, the real challenge to owning ML systems is not about the model. Sculley et al. (2015) have argued that the complexity and difficulty Compelling can be, the surrounding ecosystem- data pipelines, feature engineering, monitoring, and serving—,I think—is where most of the ongoing effort and risk lies. This paper focuses on. Such an approach has consequences. Rather than storing in the data structures themselves, it stores in the pipeline. Data management architecture to produce a consistent and exploratory design to facilitate Analysis by data scientists. And ML workflow automation. The three following sections examine various Datasets, select the appropriate one, decide on data storage solution and five-step MLOps pipeline. *Keywords: fraud detection, machine learning, MLOps data management, class imbalance.*

2. # **Candidate Source Datasets** {#candidate-source-datasets}

   1. ## **IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection** {#ieee-cis-fraud-detection}

The dataset used throughout the entire paper is the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset (Kaggle 2019\) provided by Vesta Corporation. It includes 590,540 anonymized transactions carried out via e-commerce websites, annotated by a binary variable called isFraud denoting whether the transaction was fraudulent or not. First, there is the indicator – an attribute that shows whether the corresponding transaction is a fraud. Second, an optional identity table describes a certain collection of transactions by device and network characteristics of the customer performing the transactions. Link the two tables by the TransactionID attribute. Not all transactions have an identity record, so the join operation used when ingesting the data decides on whether the data will be stored or deleted. In other words, one decides on which transactions will be part of the purchase summary which affects modeling the purchase process in nontrivial ways. The data set comes as flat CSV files and has become the go-to dataset for fraud detection. On this data,

tree-based implementations such as LightGBM easily attain AUC-ROC above 0.95. [Grinsztajn](#grinsztajn,-l.,-oyallon,-e.-&-varoquaux,-g.-\(2022\),-why-tree-based-models-still-outperform-deep-learning-on-tabular-data,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-35.-url:-https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815) [et al.](#grinsztajn,-l.,-oyallon,-e.-&-varoquaux,-g.-\(2022\),-why-tree-based-models-still-outperform-deep-learning-on-tabular-data,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-35.-url:-https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815) ([2022](#grinsztajn,-l.,-oyallon,-e.-&-varoquaux,-g.-\(2022\),-why-tree-based-models-still-outperform-deep-learning-on-tabular-data,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-35.-url:-https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815)).

**Table 1:** Aattributes of the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset

**Attribute	Description**

Transaction records	590,540 labelled e-commerce transactions

Identity records	Optional device/network data for a subset

Fraud rate	*≈*3.5% (severe class imbalance)

Total features	435 (after transaction–identity join)

Source format	Flat CSV files

![][image1]

**Figure 1:** Entity–relationship diagram for the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset

2. ## **Rossmann Store Sales** {#rossmann-store-sales}

Daily sales data of Rossmann Stores [Kaggle](#kaggle-\(2015\),-‘rossmann-store-sales’,-available-at:-https://www.kaggle.com/c/-rossmann-store-sales/data.-accessed:-10-october-2024.) ([2015](#kaggle-\(2015\),-‘rossmann-store-sales’,-available-at:-https://www.kaggle.com/c/-rossmann-store-sales/data.-accessed:-10-october-2024.)) contain daily sales amounts of over 1,000 pharmacy outlets in Germany. The task of prediction involves regression analysis, whereby sales of each store on any particular day must be predicted based on features of the store, promotions, and date-related factors. Each store has many associated daily entries with composite key

consisting of (Store, Date).

**Figure 2:** Schema for the Rossmann Store Sales dataset

3. ## **NYC TLC Trip Record Data** {#nyc-tlc-trip-record-data}

The NYC TLC Trip Record dataset ([New York City Taxi and Limousine Commission](#new-york-city-taxi-and-limousine-commission-\(2026\),-‘tlc-trip-record-data’,-available-at:) [2026](#new-york-city-taxi-and-limousine-commission-\(2026\),-‘tlc-trip-record-data’,-available-at:)) contains taxi and ride-hailing records collected for transport regulation. Monthly Parquet files are partitioned by vehicle type; each file is a flat table with no predefined ML target. The dataset is enormous — reaching billions of rows per year — and formulating a meaningful supervised learning problem would require substantial additional design work. For a prototype pipeline, this makes it far less practical than the other two options.

![][image2]

**Figure 3:** Flat-file structure of the NYC TLC Trip Record dataset

3. # **Dataset Selection** {#dataset-selection}

After reviewing all three candidates, the IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection dataset was chosen. Three reasons drive that decision. First, it comes with a clearly defined binary fraud label and reliable ground truth, unlike the NYC TLC data, which requires you to invent a target variable before any modelling can begin. Second, the optional join between transaction and identity records introduces a concrete data management decision with real downstream consequences: an in-correct inner join silently discards transactions that lack identity data, which could introduce selection bias that is impossible to correct later. Third, fraud detection naturally surfaces the MLOps challenges that matter most — concept drift, model decay, and performance monitoring  
— whereas retail sales forecasting is mainly a seasonal pattern problem with fewer pipeline implications.

**Table 2:** Dataset comparison across key selection criteria

**Criterion	IEEE-CIS	Rossmann	NYC TLC**

Defined ML target	Binary classification

Regression	Needs formulation  
Multi-entity structure	Two related tables	Simple join	Flat file only

Data quality challenges	Imbalance \+

missingness

Minor	Scale only

MLOps / drift relevance	High (fraud

adapts)

Seasonal patterns

Limited  
Manageable scale	*≈*590k rows	*≈*1M rows	Billions of rows

4. # **Data Storage Planning and Design** {#data-storage-planning-and-design}

   1. ## **Source Data Characteristics** {#source-data-characteristics}

The IEEE-CIS datasets are delivered in a basic CSV format. Although CSV is quite portable and can easily be exchanged, it is ill-suited for analysis tasks. There are approximately 435 attributes available; even a basic SQL query will have to iterate through each of those attributes on every single row, which causes a lot of unnecessary I/O operations.

2. ## **Proposed Storage Structure: Denormalised Analytical Table** {#proposed-storage-structure:-denormalised-analytical-table}

The process begins by combining the CSV files for the transaction and identities using the LEFT JOIN on TransactionID, saving the resulting denormalized Parquet file. This decision is made based on the fact that using an inner join will lead to the exclusion of any transactions without any identity data, leading to selection bias in any models generated using the dataset. Using Parquet with the Snappy compression codec leads to reduced cost of storage as well as query I/O.

**Table 3:** Comparison of storage structures for this use case

**Structure	Benefits	Drawbacks	Verdict**

Denormalised OBT (*chosen*)

ML-ready; no joins needed; fast access

Some redundancy; harder to update

Best for ML workloads  
Star Schema	BI-friendly; clean dimensions  
Joins needed for ML  
Better for BI  
Snowflake Schema	Less redundancy	Deep joins;

ML-unfriendly Normalised 3NF	High integrity	Join-heavy;  
OLTP-focused  
Unnecessary complexity Not suitable

3. ## **Ingestion Strategy: ETL vs. ELT** {#ingestion-strategy:-etl-vs.-elt}

An ETL approach was employed over ELT. While the IEEE-CIS dataset needs join-based reasoning, schema validation, and managed missing value treatment, ELT fits best for cloud data warehouses that can reliably store the raw data. ELT can work wonders in such cloud warehouses; however, storing the unprocessed and joined CSV files into a warehouse will leave all these processes to be dealt with at the ML pipeline stage, which would be more complicated to manage. By performing transformations first, only the cleaned and consistent schema is loaded into the Parquet table. Technically, the transaction and identity CSV files undergo extraction, schema validation, joining via a LEFT JOIN of the TransactionID column, type casting, treatment of missing identity columns with sentinel values, and storage of one denormalized Parquet file, compressed with Snappy. Metadata about ingestion, such as row counts, schema versions, timestamps, etc., are kept along with data.

5. # **MLOps Pipeline Design** {#mlops-pipeline-design}

The process contains five consecutive stages. All of them are detailed in accordance with the What-Why-Who-How model proposed by the assignment description.

![][image3]

**Figure 4:** MLOps pipeline overview

**Figure 5:** MLOps master pipeline — tools-based view with five stages and Apache Airflow orchestration, retraining loop driven by monitoring

1. ## **Stage 1 – Data Ingestion** {#stage-1-–-data-ingestion}

**What:** Raw CSVs are validated with schema, LEFT JOINed by TransactionID, and stored as compressed Parquet. **Why:**Lack of validation leads to errors downstream; inner join creates selection bias (Sculley et al. 2015). **Who:** Data engineers.

**How:** By Python scripts with pandas or PySpark; schema tested with Great Expectations; result stored in Parquet directory.

![][image4]

**Figure 6:** Stage 1: Data Ingestion process

2. ## **Stage 2 – Data Pre-processing** {#stage-2-–-data-pre-processing}

**What:** Data is imputed, encoding is performed on the categorical variables, and class imbalance of around 3.5 percent is dealt with.

**Why:** Imbalanced classes and data leakage are among the most frequent causes of failures in fraud models, hence they must be dealt with beforehand ([Chawla et al.](#chawla,-n.-v.,-bowyer,-k.-w.,-hall,-l.-o.-&-kegelmeyer,-w.-p.-\(2002\),-‘smote:-synthetic-minority-over-sampling-technique’,-journal-of-artificial-intelligence-research-16,-321–357.) [2002](#chawla,-n.-v.,-bowyer,-k.-w.,-hall,-l.-o.-&-kegelmeyer,-w.-p.-\(2002\),-‘smote:-synthetic-minority-over-sampling-technique’,-journal-of-artificial-intelligence-research-16,-321–357.)).

**Who:** Data scientists.

**How:** Reproducible scikit-learn pipeline; SMOTE algorithm [Chawla et al.](#chawla,-n.-v.,-bowyer,-k.-w.,-hall,-l.-o.-&-kegelmeyer,-w.-p.-\(2002\),-‘smote:-synthetic-minority-over-sampling-technique’,-journal-of-artificial-intelligence-research-16,-321–357.) ([2002](#chawla,-n.-v.,-bowyer,-k.-w.,-hall,-l.-o.-&-kegelmeyer,-w.-p.-\(2002\),-‘smote:-synthetic-minority-over-sampling-technique’,-journal-of-artificial-intelligence-research-16,-321–357.)); stratified splitting of the train-test set.

**Figure 7:** Stage 2: Data Pre-processing process

3. ## **Stage 3 – Model Development** {#stage-3-–-model-development}

**What:** Training, evaluation, and explanation of a binary classifier based on LightGBM.

**Why:** Because tree-based models are proven to work better than deep learning models on datasets like this one [Grinsztajn et al.](#grinsztajn,-l.,-oyallon,-e.-&-varoquaux,-g.-\(2022\),-why-tree-based-models-still-outperform-deep-learning-on-tabular-data,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-35.-url:-https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815) ([2022](#grinsztajn,-l.,-oyallon,-e.-&-varoquaux,-g.-\(2022\),-why-tree-based-models-still-outperform-deep-learning-on-tabular-data,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-35.-url:-https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815)), while SHAP makes explainability more understandable from a business perspective.

**Who:** Data scientists.

**How:** Stratified k-fold cross-validation; AUC-ROC used as a key metric; all experiments logged in MLflow; SHAP values calculated for the best model.

**Figure 8:** Stage 3: Model Development process

4. ## **Stage 4 – Model Deployment** {#stage-4-–-model-deployment}

**What:** The model training, preprocessing pipeline, and inference logic are then packaged and registered.

**Why:** In absence of versioned artifacts and staging checkpoints, reverting to a previous version of a flawed model in production is both difficult and dangerous ([Kreuzberger et al.](#kreuzberger,-d.,-kühl,-n.-&-hirschl,-s.-\(2023\),-‘machine-learning-operations-\(mlops\):-overview,-definition,-and-architecture’,-ieee-access-11,-31866–31879.) [2023](#kreuzberger,-d.,-kühl,-n.-&-hirschl,-s.-\(2023\),-‘machine-learning-operations-\(mlops\):-overview,-definition,-and-architecture’,-ieee-access-11,-31866–31879.)).

**Who:** ML engineers.

**How:** MLflow Model Registry with stages such as Staging, Production, and Archived; batch inference initially, followed by REST endpoint possibility.

![][image5]

**Figure 9:** Stage 4: Model Deployment process

5. ## **Stage 5 – Model Monitoring** {#stage-5-–-model-monitoring}

**What:** Model effectiveness and feature distributions are constantly monitored post-deployment.

**Why:** Fraudsters are always changing their ways; if a model fails to adapt, it will gradually deteriorate [Sculley et al.](#sculley,-d.,-holt,-g.,-golovin,-d.,-davydov,-e.,-phillips,-t.,-ebner,-d.,-chaudhary,-v.,-young,-m.,-crespo,-j.-f.-&-dennison,-d.-\(2015\),-hidden-technical-debt-in-machine-learning-systems,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-28,-pp.-2503–2511.) ([2015](#sculley,-d.,-holt,-g.,-golovin,-d.,-davydov,-e.,-phillips,-t.,-ebner,-d.,-chaudhary,-v.,-young,-m.,-crespo,-j.-f.-&-dennison,-d.-\(2015\),-hidden-technical-debt-in-machine-learning-systems,-in-‘advances-in-neural-information-processing-systems’,-vol.-28,-pp.-2503–2511.)). Monitoring concept drift facilitates timely retraining of the model.

**Who:** ML engineers.

**How:**Monitoring PSI values and feature drift using statistical hypothesis tests; monitoring AUC-ROC value of the labeled batches; retraining is triggered when the AUC-ROC score drops below a certain level.

**Figure 10:** Stage 5: Model Monitoring process

6. # **Reflection** {#reflection}

Working through this exercise has definitely changed my mind on the idea of dataset selection. Initially, I thought of it as an administrative task to be accomplished quickly before we move on to design. Once I started exploring the way the datasets joined together, I realised just how much this step impacts what we end up with. Had we opted for an inner join from the get-go, a good chunk of our training population would just be gone – and there is no way we would correct for that bias later down the line. Now, I understand that selecting and joining our dataset is also something that needs design consideration. The exercise helped me distinguish between two concepts which I confused initially. Parquet is a physical storage format and does not necessarily imply a certain logical database structure. Parquet can store both denormalised one-big-table structures and star schemas equally well. This distinction simplified a lot of arguments for our selected solution and made it independent of storage type. Finally, I wish I identified the data consumers earlier in the design phase. Interactive analysis and inference by machine learning models make vastly different requirements of our storage solution. Being aware of this would help develop more interesting discussions on the topic. Going forward, this report will definitely evolve as more feedback emerges.

7. # **Further Plans** {#further-plans}

Immediate follow-up actions include setting up an ETL pipeline in Python with schema and row count validations as well as carrying out exploratory analysis on the IEEE-CIS dataset. Preprocessing will be established through a scikit-learn Pipeline in order to ensure repeatability during model training. A first baseline will be created with LightGBM, employing k-fold stratification in each experiment, and logging everything into MLflow. Along the way, any realistic constraint identified during development will lead to adjusting this approach.

# **7	References**

Chawla, N. V., Bowyer, K. W., Hall, L. O. & Kegelmeyer, W. P. (2002), ‘SMOTE: Synthetic minority over-sampling technique’, *Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research* **16**, 321–357.

Grinsztajn, L., Oyallon, E. & Varoquaux, G. (2022), Why tree-based models still outperform deep learning on tabular data, *in* ‘Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems’, Vol. 35\. **URL:** *[https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815*](https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.08815)

Kaggle (2015), ‘Rossmann store sales’, Available at:	[https://www.kaggle.com/c/](https://www.kaggle.com/c/rossmann-store-sales/data) [rossmann-store-sales/data](https://www.kaggle.com/c/rossmann-store-sales/data). Accessed: 10 October 2024\.

Kreuzberger, D., Kühl, N. & Hirschl, S. (2023), ‘Machine learning operations (MLOps): Overview, definition, and architecture’, *IEEE Access* **11**, 31866–31879.

New York City Taxi and Limousine Commission (2026), ‘TLC trip record data’, Available at:

[https://www.nyc.gov/site/tlc/about/tlc-trip-record-data.page](https://www.nyc.gov/site/tlc/about/tlc-trip-record-data.page). Accessed: 10

October 2024\.

Sculley, D., Holt, G., Golovin, D., Davydov, E., Phillips, T., Ebner, D., Chaudhary, V., Young, M., Crespo, J.-F. & Dennison, D. (2015), Hidden technical debt in machine learning systems, *in* ‘Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems’, Vol. 28, pp. 2503–2511.
